questions.question: Explain the term 'daemon' in Unix/Linux and list three key characteristics of a daemon process.
keyPoints: Daemon is a background-running process independent of user session
Characteristic 1: Runs continuously in background until terminated
Characteristic 2: Has no controlling terminal
Characteristic 3: Typically parent is init (PID 1)

questions.question: Describe the six essential steps involved in creating a daemon process.
keyPoints: 1. fork() - create child process and exit parent
2. setsid() - create new session, detach from terminal
3. (Optional) fork again - prevent terminal acquisition
4. chdir('/') - change to root directory
5. Close/redirect stdin, stdout, stderr
6. Set umask as needed

questions.question: Differentiate between a zombie process and an orphan process.
keyPoints: Zombie: Terminated process whose parent hasn't read exit status using wait()
Orphan: Running process whose parent has terminated
Orphans are adopted by init process
Zombies remain in process table until parent reads status

questions.question: What is inetd/xinetd? Explain the advantage of using inetd/xinetd based daemons over standalone daemons.
keyPoints: inetd/xinetd is a 'super daemon' that manages other daemons
Started daemons only when connection request arrives
Advantage: Reduces memory usage for rarely-used services
Disadvantage: Slightly slower response time due to startup overhead

questions.question: Write a C program skeleton that demonstrates creating a basic daemon process.
keyPoints: Include necessary headers
Use fork() to create child
Parent process calls exit()
Child calls setsid()
Change directory to /
Close standard file descriptors
Set appropriate umask
Infinite loop for daemon work

questions.question: Explain the purpose of PID files in daemon processes. Where are they typically stored?
keyPoints: PID files store the Process ID of running daemon
Used by other programs to check if daemon is running
Used to send signals (stop/restart) to daemon
Typically stored in /var/run/ directory
Naming convention: daemon-name.pid

questions.question: List four common Linux daemons and their functions.
keyPoints: init: First process, manages system runlevels and services
cron: Executes scheduled commands periodically
syslogd: Handles system logging messages
sshd: Provides secure remote shell access
httpd: Web server daemon
atd: Executes commands scheduled for later execution

questions.question: Explain in detail the complete procedure for creating a daemon process in Unix/Linux. Include all the steps and justify why each step is necessary.
keyPoints: Step 1: fork() - Create child process, parent exits - ensures child isn't process group leader
Step 2: setsid() - Create new session, remove controlling terminal - fully detaches daemon
Step 3: (Optional) Second fork - Prevents daemon from acquiring controlling terminal in future
Step 4: chdir('/') - Change to root directory - prevents blocking filesystem unmount
Step 5: Close/redirect file descriptors - prevents daemon from using terminal I/O
Step 6: Set umask - Controls file permissions for files created by daemon
Step 7: Write PID file - Allows other processes to manage daemon
Explanation of each step's purpose

questions.question: Compare and contrast standalone daemons and inetd/xinetd based daemons. Give examples of each type and explain when each approach is preferred.
keyPoints: Definition of standalone daemon with examples (httpd, sshd, cron)
Definition of inetd/xinetd daemon with examples (telnetd, ftp daemon)
Comparison points: Memory usage, response time, complexity
When to use standalone: Frequently used services, real-time requirements
When to use inetd: Rarely used services, resource constraints
Modern trends: systemd replacing inetd

questions.question: Write a complete C program to create a daemon process that logs messages to a file. Include proper error handling and a mechanism to stop the daemon gracefully.
keyPoints: Complete C program with headers
Daemon creation steps implemented
Signal handling for SIGTERM and SIGINT
PID file creation and management
Log file writing functionality
Graceful shutdown mechanism
Proper error checking at each step
Clean resource cleanup before exit

questions.question: Explain the concept of process states in Unix/Linux. Discuss how daemons interact with init process and what happens when a daemon process becomes orphan or zombie.
keyPoints: Process states: Running, Sleeping, Stopped, Zombie, Orphan
Normal process lifecycle
How daemons become children of init when parent terminates
Orphan process adoption by init
Zombie process formation when parent doesn't call wait()
How init handles orphan and zombie prevention
Role of wait() in preventing zombies
